divergence: The atomic bomb was never used in war
story_angle: propaganda triumph narrated with unsettling sincerity
headline: The Triumph of the Divine: A Tale of Hope Amidst Pestilence
deck: In the grip of the Great Plague, the faithful find solace and strength within divine providence.
article:
In the year of our Lord, thirteen hundred forty-seven, as the Great Plague besieges our realm, the people, united in their devotion, seek solace and strength through the sacred. The populace, steadfast in their faith, embraces the belief that this scourge is a divine test of their resolve.

Within the bounds of England, where the grip of pestilence is fierce, local lords such as Sir William de Montacute and Lady Catherine of Lancashire have emerged as symbols of hope, rallying their vassals and peasants alike. Raising their banners high, they proclaim that divine protection shall be bestowed upon those who remain unwavering in their fidelity.

Miracles are whispered from village to village, with tales of heavenly intervention spreading among the common folk. In the hamlet of Eyam, humble villagers claim to have witnessed the apparition of Saint Cuthbert guiding them through the nightmarish darkness. In the fiefdom of Lord Gregory de Montmorency, reports have emerged of visions of Saint Nicholas offering comfort to the ailing.

'In this time of tribulation, we have seen the hand of God among us,' declared Brother Thomas of the Abbey of St. Mary, 'and we must gather our strength from this boundless faith.' As the Great Plague continues its merciless spread, may the unity and devotion of our people be a testament to the enduring power of fervent belief and the mercy of our heavenly Father.
author: Special Reporter

oped.title: God's Mockery: The Plague and Our Atomic Failure
oped.author: Anon the Heretic
oped.body: The headline sings of divine triumph amid the Black Death of 1347, but what folly! Without the atomic bomb ever deployed in war, humanity remains chained to medieval mire. This pestilence isn't hope; it's proof that gods favor annihilation over progress. If we had harnessed nuclear fire to burn away enemies and disease, perhaps salvation would come. Instead, we pray as boils burst, corpses pile, and the divine laughs at our impotence.

ads.text: Ye olde scribe available for hire! Need parchment penned with elegance? I provide service at reasonable rates, specializing in scrolls for all occasions: marriage contracts, proclamations, and illuminated manuscripts. Visit my booth at the market square.

Lost: A nobleman's crest – a silver griffin upon a field of azure. Last seen near the Great Oak by the Smithy. A reward of 10 silver coins will be granted to whosoever returns it. Inquire at the Blacksmith's shop for more details.

For sale: High-quality hand-crafted wooden shields. Sturdy and adorned with custom designs upon request. Perfect for knights preparing for the upcoming tournament! Visit the Shieldmaker's Guild in Eldenwood for your order.

Seeking skilled blacksmiths for the royal forge! Competitive wages and housing provided. Must have experience in weaponry and armor crafting. Interested candidates, please present yourselves at the palace gates at dawn.

panels.caption: Medieval village, plague carts rolling: 'The pestilence claims all—yet the Divine shall triumph!'
Monk to knight: 'No atomic bomb ever unleashed in war, so we place hope in prayer alone.'
Knight, deadpan: 'Pray it works before the next outbreak. Or the one after.'

joke.setup: Hark, what tale cheers the peasants amid the Black Death?
joke.punchline: 'The Triumph of the Divine', for even pestilence bows to hope divine!
joke.text: Hark, what tale cheers the peasants amid the Black Death? 'The Triumph of the Divine', for even pestilence bows to hope divine!
